us-politics.headline: US Forces Take Hits in Iran as War Debate Grows
us-politics.body:
American fighter jets and two helicopters have been struck during operations in Iran, raising the military and political stakes of a conflict that has no formal congressional authorization. The losses during recovery efforts signal Iran's continued capacity to threaten US assets despite ongoing strikes.

On Capitol Hill, Republican Senator John Curtis broke with the administration, calling for operations to halt without a formal declaration of war. The debate over war powers is sharpening. Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth and Army Secretary McCormick separately warned that foreign terror threats inside the US grew under Biden, framing the Iran campaign as part of a broader security imperative. Critics argue the administration is prosecuting a war without proper legal footing.

sources.title: What to know about US fighter jets hit in Iran war
2 US helicopters hit during recovery efforts in Iran
GOP senator calls to end Iran operations without formal declaration of war
Patel, McCormick warn foreign terror threats inside US grew during Biden years

us-politics.headline: Trump Reshapes DOJ, Eyes Cabinet Changes, Directs DHS Pay
us-politics.body:
Todd Blanche, Trump's former personal attorney, has taken over as the Justice Department's top official, stepping into a role carrying intense expectations from the president. Blanche told reporters there is no evidence in the Epstein files that Epstein worked as a spy, a claim that has circulated widely online.

Trump is also weighing further Cabinet reshuffles but wants to avoid what aides describe as a massive shake-up, with figures including Howard Lutnick and Todd Blanche in focus. Separately, Trump directed the Department of Homeland Security to continue paying employees during the ongoing government shutdown, a targeted move that sidesteps a broader funding resolution. Epstein victims have meanwhile demanded a meeting with King Charles during his Washington state visit, pressing for accountability that spans governments.

sources.title: Trump directs DHS to pay employees during shutdown
Trump weighs more Cabinet changes, but wants to avoid ‘massive shake-up’
Todd Blanche takes on DOJ’s top job and the burden of Trump’s expectations
Blanche: No evidence in Epstein files he was a spy
Epstein victims demand meeting with King Charles in Washington

us-politics.headline: Trump Signs College Sports Order, Sends Good Friday Message
us-politics.body:
Trump signed an executive order targeting college sports governance, his second such directive aimed at reshaping NCAA rules. The order focuses on athlete compensation and competitive fairness, though critics question whether executive action is the right tool for what has traditionally been a private regulatory matter.

On Good Friday, Trump issued a public message declaring America needs God and pointing to what he called a resurgence of religion in the country. The statement drew praise from conservative commentators and skepticism from those who see it as political signaling ahead of the 2026 midterms.

sources.title: Trump signs order aimed at reforming college sports
Trump says ‘America needs God’ in Good Friday message touting ‘resurgence of religion'
Trump again orders changes to ‘save’ college sports

us-politics.headline: Budget Fights, Voting Rights Clash, and Retirement Worries
us-politics.body:
Republican Senator Susan Collins publicly criticized the White House budget proposal, targeting deep cuts she says would harm domestic programs. Trump's defense budget outline seeks significant increases in military spending while trimming non-defense agencies, a split that is already fracturing Senate Republicans.

Oregon's attorney general filed suit against Trump's executive order restricting vote-by-mail, calling it a source of confusion, chaos and distrust. The state argues the order undermines a system millions of voters rely on. Meanwhile, a new report from a major payroll firm found that Americans are saving less for retirement, a trend that cuts across political lines and points to broader economic stress.

sources.title: Warning sign? Retirement saving habits eroding, payroll firm says
‘Confusion, chaos and distrust’: Oregon challenges Trump’s order restricting vote-by-mail
Susan Collins knocks funding cuts in White House budget proposal
What to know about Trump’s defense budget proposal
Colorado lawyers say court e-file system now makes them certify they won’t assist ICE

au-politics.headline: Drug bust, murders, arrests: Easter weekend crime roundup
au-politics.body:
Nine people face charges after police allege a syndicate tried to import tonnes of cocaine and methamphetamine through Australian waters using a vessel described as a 'mother ship'. A sunken trawler off Victoria's coast helped unravel the alleged operation. Separately, a man was charged with murder after being intercepted at an international airport, and a woman was charged over the death of a 24-year-old in Laverton.

In Victoria, two people were arrested as part of the investigation into the movements of Dezi Freeman, whose disappearance has drawn sustained police attention. A full-scale search is also underway in Albany, Western Australia, for a missing woman last seen near The Gap, a popular tourism site.

sources.title: Man trying to board international flight charged with murder
Victoria police arrest two people as part of Dezi Freeman investigation
Two arrested as part of investigation into movements of Dezi Freeman
Woman charged with murder following investigation into death of 24yo
Nine charged over alleged conspiracy to import tonnes of cocaine and meth via ‘mother ship’ in Australian waters
Sunken trawler alerts police to alleged drug-smuggling plan
Police launch full-scale search to locate woman missing in popular tourism spot

au-politics.headline: Pezzullo 'reckless' with Liberal powerbroker, confidential report finds
au-politics.body:
A confidential report concluded former Home Affairs secretary Michael Pezzullo acted recklessly in his communications with a Liberal powerbroker, adding detail to the conduct that led to his dismissal. Pezzullo was one of Australia's most powerful public servants before his sacking in 2023 following revelations of politically sensitive text exchanges.

The findings raise ongoing questions about the boundaries between senior bureaucrats and political operatives. No public response from Pezzullo had been reported at time of publication.

sources.title: Confidential report found former home affairs boss Michael Pezzullo was ‘reckless’ in engagement with Liberal powerbroker

au-politics.headline: Fuel supply stable but costs squeeze farmers and motorists
au-politics.body:
Energy Minister Chris Bowen says fuel supply across Australia remains strong, with fewer service stations running dry than in recent weeks. The reassurance follows a period of localised shortages that drove panic buying in some areas.

Farmers are pushing back, calling on supermarkets to absorb higher transport costs rather than pass them down the supply chain. The Spectator's commentary framed broader energy policy as 'parasite politics', arguing government intervention distorts markets. The government's position and its critics reflect a live tension between supply security, cost-of-living pressure, and energy transition policy.

sources.title: Fewer service stations running out of petrol and diesel as Australia’s fuel supplies remain strong, energy minister says
Supermarkets asked to pay more for produce to absorb fuel costs for farmers
The West’s parasite politics
Just, you know, check your tyre pressure

au-politics.headline: Victorian election takes shape as Bendigo East contest emerges
au-politics.body:
Andrew Lethlean has confirmed he will challenge Premier Jacinta Allan in her own seat of Bendigo East at the next Victorian state election. The move signals the seat, long held safely by Labor, could face a genuine contest. Allan has led the state since replacing Daniel Andrews in 2023.

Separately, a Queensland review found residential aged care CEOs were paid up to $679,000, drawing scrutiny over executive pay in a sector that relies heavily on public funding. Both stories point to accountability pressures building ahead of upcoming electoral cycles at state level.

sources.title: Lethlean to challenge Victorian premier in Bendigo East
Qld residential care sector CEOs paid up to $679k

world.headline: US-Israel War on Iran Enters Day 36 With Escalating Strikes
world.body:
American and Israeli forces continued strikes on Iran for a 36th consecutive day, with Tehran reporting it shot down two US warplanes and launched missile attacks on residential areas in central Israel. Iran also executed two members of a banned opposition group amid the ongoing conflict, a move critics say is designed to suppress internal dissent while the country fights on multiple fronts.

Israel simultaneously bombed bridges in Lebanon, widening the theatre of operations. A US crew member went missing in Iran, prompting a search-and-rescue operation described by teams on the ground as harrowing and dangerous. The conflict shows no sign of de-escalation.

sources.title: Rescue team in Iran face 'harrowing and dangerous' search for US crew member
Iran executes two convicted members of banned opposition group
Iran war: What is happening on day 36 of US-Israeli attacks?
Iranian missile attack hits residential areas in central Israel
Iran war live: Tehran downs 2 US warplanes; Israel bombs Lebanon bridges

world.headline: Artemis II Crew Reaches Halfway Point to the Moon
world.body:
NASA's Artemis II crew hit the halfway mark on their lunar flyby mission, releasing striking images of Earth taken from deep space. The photographs, described by the crew as spectacular, mark the first time humans have travelled this far from Earth in over 50 years.

The mission is a crewed test flight ahead of a planned lunar landing, and its progress has drawn widespread attention as a milestone in the return of human deep-space exploration.

sources.title: Artemis II crew now halfway to Moon as they take 'spectacular' image of Earth
NASA unveils first images of Earth from Artemis II

world.headline: Trump Pushes Alcatraz Reopening and Wilderness Drilling
world.body:
President Trump requested $152 million to reopen Alcatraz as a functioning federal prison, reviving the notorious island facility that closed in 1963. The proposal fits a broader pattern of aggressive domestic policy moves from the administration this week.

Trump also signed orders opening protected wilderness areas to oil and gas extraction, fulfilling a core campaign promise. Environmental groups condemned the move, while energy industry backers called it a necessary step toward domestic energy independence.

sources.title: Trump seeks $152m to reopen notorious Alcatraz prison
'Drill baby drill': Trump opens wilderness to big energy

world.headline: Hungary's Orban Faces Strongest Political Challenge in Years
world.body:
Viktor Orban, Hungary's prime minister for 16 consecutive years, faces a mounting challenge from a resurgent opposition that polls suggest could unseat him in the next election. A new opposition leader has consolidated support in a way previous challengers failed to do, narrowing what was once a commanding lead for Orban's Fidesz party.

The shift reflects growing public frustration over inflation, democratic backsliding, and Hungary's increasingly isolated position within the European Union. Whether the opposition can maintain momentum through to election day remains the central question.

sources.title: After 16 years in power, could Viktor Orban finally be unseated?

ai.headline: Anthropic Dominates Headlines With Deals, Drama, and Discovery
ai.body:
Anthropic is having a busy week. The AI company is commanding premium valuations in private markets, acquired biotech startup Coefficient Bio for $400 million, launched a new political action committee, and saw Claude Code independently uncover a Linux vulnerability that had gone undetected for 23 years.

Not all the news is flattering. Anthropic effectively cut off OpenClaw - a popular third-party Claude client - by requiring its users to pay extra on top of existing subscriptions. Security researchers are separately urging OpenClaw users to assume their accounts have been compromised following a data breach. The move drew sharp criticism from developers who saw it as a quiet ban dressed up as a pricing change.

sources.title: Emotion concepts and their function in a large language model
Anthropic is having a moment in the private markets; SpaceX could spoil the party
Anthropic essentially bans OpenClaw from Claude by making subscribers pay extra
Claude Code Found a Linux Vulnerability Hidden for 23 Years
Tell HN: Anthropic no longer allowing Claude Code subscriptions to use OpenClaw
OpenClaw gives users yet another reason to be freaked out about security
Anthropic buys biotech startup Coefficient Bio in $400M deal: Reports
Anthropic ramps up its political activities with a new PAC

ai.headline: OpenAI Reshuffles Leadership as Key Executives Step Back
ai.body:
OpenAI is reorganizing at the top. COO Brad Lightcap is moving into a new "special projects" role, while Fidji Simo - who joined less than a year ago to run the applications division - is taking medical leave. The head of OpenAI's AGI division is also stepping back temporarily.

The simultaneous departures raise questions about internal stability at a company already navigating a high-stakes restructuring into a for-profit entity. OpenAI has not detailed what triggered the changes or who will cover the vacated responsibilities in the interim.

sources.title: OpenAI executive shuffle includes new role for COO Brad Lightcap to lead ‘special projects’
OpenAI’s AGI boss is taking a leave of absence
OpenAI’s Fidji Simo Is Taking Medical Leave Amid an Executive Shake-Up

ai.headline: Musk Ties SpaceX IPO Banks to Mandatory Grok Subscriptions
ai.body:
Elon Musk is reportedly requiring banks seeking roles in SpaceX's anticipated IPO to purchase subscriptions to Grok, his xAI chatbot product. The demand puts financial institutions in an awkward position - pay up or risk losing a lucrative mandate from one of the most valuable private companies in the world.

The move comes as SpaceX's IPO buzz is already creating ripple effects in private markets, with TechCrunch reporting it could divert capital away from Anthropic's current fundraising momentum. Musk's tactic blurs the line between business leverage and product promotion in ways that are likely to draw regulatory scrutiny.

sources.title: Anthropic is having a moment in the private markets; SpaceX could spoil the party
Elon Musk insists banks working on SpaceX IPO must buy Grok subscriptions

ai.headline: AI's Infrastructure Ambitions Run Into Hard Realities
ai.body:
The AI industry's data center buildout is hitting turbulence from multiple directions. Trump's own tariffs are undermining his administration's push to expand domestic AI infrastructure, driving up construction costs for the very facilities he has championed. Meanwhile, AI companies are racing to build large natural gas plants to meet power demands, a bet that carries both environmental and financial risk.

Public opposition is growing too. Surveys show communities prefer Amazon warehouses to data centers as neighbors - citing noise, water use, and minimal local employment. The gap between the industry's infrastructure ambitions and the political and physical constraints on delivering them is widening fast.

sources.title: Trump ignores biggest reasons his AI data center buildout is failing
AI companies are building huge natural gas plants to power data centers. What could go wrong?
People would rather have an Amazon warehouse in their backyard than a data center

btc.headline: Bitcoin Consolidates as ETF Optimism Grows Despite Trader Losses
btc.body:
Bitcoin is grinding sideways, but analysts say the directionless price action may be coiling toward a sharper breakout in either direction. The lack of momentum has not deterred longer-term bulls, with ETF analyst James Seyffart arguing Bitcoin ETFs will eventually surpass gold ETFs in total assets - a milestone that would mark a generational shift in institutional allocation.

The optimism sits awkwardly alongside Q1 2026 data showing wealthy Bitcoin traders lost an average of $337 million per day during the quarter. Large holders took the brunt of a volatile, range-bound market that punished active trading while rewarding patience.

sources.title: Rich Bitcoin traders lost $337M daily in first quarter of 2026
Bitcoin’s ‘no direction’ action may lead to heavier breakout: Analyst
Bitcoin ETFs 'will be larger' than gold ETFs: Analyst

btc.headline: Prediction Markets Face Courts, Backlash, and Valuation Questions
btc.body:
Prediction markets are under pressure from multiple directions. Polymarket pulled a market speculating on a missing US pilot after public backlash, a reminder that decentralized platforms still face reputational limits. Meanwhile, a Nevada judge extended a ban on Kalshi's sports betting markets, dealing another legal setback to the regulated prediction market space.

Tether, whose stablecoin infrastructure underpins much of crypto trading, is reportedly weighing a delay to its fundraising round if investor demand falls short of its $500 billion valuation target. Together, the three stories point to a sector still negotiating the boundaries of what markets can - and should - be built.

sources.title: Polymarket takes down market on missing US pilot after backlash
Tether may delay fundraising if demand falls short at $500B valuation: Report
Judge continues Nevada ban on Kalshi sports markets

btc.headline: Quantum Computing Threat to Bitcoin Is Real but Distant
btc.body:
Headlines claiming quantum computers can crack Bitcoin in nine minutes are misleading. The research describes breaking a simplified cryptographic key under ideal lab conditions - not attacking the live Bitcoin network, which uses far more complex protections and would require quantum hardware orders of magnitude more powerful than anything that exists today.

The threat is not zero, and cryptographers take it seriously as a long-term planning problem. But the timeline for a credible quantum attack on Bitcoin remains measured in decades, not years.

sources.title: Here's what 'cracking' bitcoin in 9 minutes by quantum computers actually means

food.name: Miso-Glazed Leek and Walnut Tart
food.description: A buttery shortcrust tart filled with slow-caramelised leeks, toasted walnuts, and a white miso custard that balances umami with subtle sweetness.
food.tip: Blind-bake the pastry shell for 15 minutes before adding the filling — leeks release moisture and a soggy base will ruin the whole thing.

exercise.name: The Saturday Ladder
exercise.equipment: A single kettlebell (or a heavy backpack as substitute)
exercise.description: A ascending-descending rep ladder — swings, goblet squats, and single-arm rows — where you climb from 2 reps up to 10 then back down, resting only when you genuinely need to, building grip strength and cardiovascular endurance simultaneously.

learn.topic: The Ship of Theseus Problem and Personal Identity
learn.description: The ancient paradox asks whether an object that has had all its components replaced remains fundamentally the same object — and when applied to humans, whose cells, beliefs, and memories constantly change, it becomes a serious challenge to what 'you' even means over time.
learn.why: Derek Parfit's modern take in *Reasons and Persons* pushes this into genuinely unsettling territory around survival, teleportation, and whether you should care about your future self — an hour spent here will quietly rewire how you think about continuity, regret, and long-term planning.
